title: When will language models be good enough?
gist: Colin Raffel argues language model scaling may follow the same arc as supersonic aircraft: impressive for a while, then abandoned once exponential cost outpaces the value of further gains, with 'good enough' small/efficient models and better harnesses potentially mattering more than frontier scale.

themes.theme: Scaling cost trajectory vs. supersonic aircraft parable

insights.claim: Boeing's commercial supersonic aircraft program was abandoned because the cost, complexity, and noise of supersonic flight were not worth the benefit of a faster transatlantic trip, even though pre-747 trends suggested speed and capacity would keep increasing indefinitely; language model scaling could follow the same arc of diminishing practical payoff.
insights.speaker: Colin Raffel
insights.so_what: Watch for the point where the marginal capability gained from further scaling stops justifying its cost, just as supersonic commercial flight was scrapped despite technical feasibility.
insights.quote: it is just too expensive to build and fly supersonic airplanes, not to mention the loud booming noises they make. Halving the duration of a transatlantic flight is just not worth the dramatically increased cost and inconvenience.

insights.claim: Model training costs are increasing by roughly 5x each year, according to Epoch AI's analysis, meaning continued frontier scaling requires exponential rather than linear investment growth.
insights.speaker: Colin Raffel
insights.so_what: A 5x/year cost curve is unsustainable for most balance sheets long-term; investors should track how many more scaling cycles current AI labs can actually fund.
insights.quote: Epoch AI's analysis suggests that model training costs are increasing by roughly 5x each year.

insights.claim: OpenAI's leaked financial documents reportedly show training costs of roughly $10 billion for frontier models, raising doubts about the financial sustainability of continued scaling.
insights.speaker: Colin Raffel
insights.so_what: A $10B-per-model cost floor concentrates frontier training to a handful of capital-rich players and raises the bar for ROI on each new model generation.
insights.quote: OpenAI's leaked financial documents suggest training costs of roughly $10B and, more broadly, a worrying picture for the financial sustainability of continued scaling.

themes.theme: Diminishing practical returns from frontier capability

insights.claim: Simon Willison recounted an episode where the AI system Fable spent an inordinate amount of time and compute building a custom debugging framework and screenshot tooling just to ultimately produce a two-line CSS fix, suggesting a 'weaker' model paired with the right tooling would have solved the task more efficiently.
insights.speaker: Colin Raffel
insights.so_what: Beyond a certain point, added model capability may just enable more expensive default behavior (over-engineering) rather than better outcomes; system/tooling design can substitute for raw capability.
insights.quote: Fable ultimately spent an inordinate amount of time and compute building a custom framework for running the app and taking screenshots before it ultimately produced a two-line CSS change to fix the bug.
insights.locator: Paragraph beginning 'Simon Willison tells an interesting story'

insights.claim: For most everyday use cases, frontier-level capabilities such as proving advanced mathematical conjectures are irrelevant, since typical requests (e.g., a taco recipe) don't require anywhere near that level of sophistication.
insights.speaker: Colin Raffel
insights.so_what: Frontier benchmark gains may not translate into value for the bulk of commercial/consumer use cases, arguing against paying a premium for frontier models across all applications.
insights.quote: Do we really need our models to be able to (dis)prove Erdős conjectures when we ask them to give us a taco recipe?
insights.locator: Paragraph beginning 'Simon Willison tells an interesting story'

themes.theme: Good-enough small models and efficiency gains

insights.claim: Recent open-weights models, when harnessed correctly, are reportedly good enough to serve as useful coding agents, approaching the utility of late-2025 frontier closed models while running locally on modest consumer hardware.
insights.speaker: Colin Raffel
insights.so_what: If smaller open models already match frontier utility for coding tasks, dependence on paid frontier APIs for that use case may shrink, favoring local/open-source deployment.
insights.quote: recent open-weights models, when harnessed correctly, are good enough to be useful coding agents, approaching the utility of late-2025 "frontier" closed models while being runnable locally on modest consumer hardware
insights.locator: Paragraph beginning 'Even "small" models far from the "frontier"'

insights.claim: The recently developed 'AI worm' reportedly runs on a mediocre, undisclosed local model wrapped in a sophisticated harness, illustrating that a well-engineered system around a modest model can be highly effective without frontier capability.
insights.speaker: Colin Raffel
insights.so_what: Effective (including malicious) autonomous AI systems don't require frontier models, lowering the capability bar for building impactful agents -- a relevant security and competitive-moat consideration.
insights.quote: the recently developed AI worm is, apparently, a somewhat mediocre (albeit undisclosed for security reasons) local model, surrounded by what sounds like a sophisticated harness.
insights.locator: Paragraph beginning 'Even "small" models far from the "frontier"'

insights.claim: Epoch AI estimates that the amount of compute required to reach a given level of model performance has roughly halved every 8 months, implying today's frontier-level performance could become achievable on much smaller/cheaper models within a few years.
insights.speaker: Colin Raffel
insights.so_what: An 8-month compute-efficiency halving curve means efficiency/distillation research, not just raw scale, is a key lever for cost reduction -- worth tracking alongside training-cost growth.
insights.quote: Epoch AI estimate that the amount of compute required to attain a certain level of performance has roughly halved every 8 months.
insights.locator: Paragraph beginning 'Even "small" models far from the "frontier"'

themes.theme: Proposed direction: shrink models, don't just scale

insights.claim: Raffel suggests the most prudent path for language model development going forward may not be continued scaling, but rather redoubling efforts to shrink 'good enough' models while building better systems ('managing a genius') around them.
insights.speaker: Colin Raffel
insights.so_what: Signals a possible reallocation of R&D and investment priority away from raw pretraining scale toward efficiency, distillation, and agent-harness engineering.
insights.quote: Perhaps the most prudent path for language model development is not scaling, but rather redoubling our efforts to shrink down good-enough models.
